title: SEGMENT 2 — The workhorses: who actually does the work inside a fermenter

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — The workhorses: who actually does the work inside a fermenter
Before we engineer anything, we need to know which cells we are engineering.
Because here is the thing — and I want you to feel the weight of it — you do not get to pick any organism off the tree of life and use it as your metabolic factory.
You pick from a very short list.
The list of organisms that have been domesticated to the point where they can be reliably engineered and grown at industrial scale is, even today, maybe ten species deep.
Four of those species do something like ninety percent of the world's biomanufacturing.
Those four are our workhorses, and we need to spend some time on each, because the choice of workhorse shapes every downstream decision.
The first workhorse, and the one you will hear about most often, is **Escherichia coli**.
E. coli is the lab rat of microbiology.
It is a gram-negative bacterium, normally found in the gut of mammals, harmless in its lab-adapted strains.
It grows fast — doubling every twenty minutes under good conditions, which means a single cell can produce a fermenter full of biomass in less than a day.

It is genetically tractable, which is a polite way of saying we have been engineering it for fifty years and have built up an enormous toolkit — well-characterized promoters, dozens of selectable markers, every kind of plasmid you could want, CRISPR, MAGE, recombineering, all the toys.
The first ever recombinant protein drug — insulin — was produced in E. coli, in 1978.
E. coli is fast, transformable, cheap to grow on simple media, and easy to scale up.
Its weaknesses are real but specific.
It is a prokaryote, so it cannot do the post-translational modifications — glycosylation, complex disulfide arrangements, proteolytic processing — that some eukaryotic proteins require.
It can have trouble with very long proteins.
It can dump misfolded protein into insoluble inclusion bodies that you then have to refold.
For small molecules and for simpler proteins, though, E. coli is the default choice, and a fermenter full of E. coli is the workhorse setup of the industry.
The second workhorse is **Saccharomyces cerevisiae** — baker's yeast, brewer's yeast, the yeast that has fermented bread and beer for ten thousand years.
S. cerevisiae is a eukaryote, which is the key fact about it.
It has a nucleus, it has an endoplasmic reticulum, it has Golgi, it has the full set of organelles and the full set of eukaryotic post-translational machinery.
Proteins that need to be glycosylated, that need complex folding, that need to be secreted — yeast can often do those when E. coli cannot.
Yeast is also what is called GRAS in the regulatory world, which stands for "generally recognized as safe.
" Because humans have been eating yeast forever, the regulatory bar for using yeast in food and pharmaceutical applications is lower than for many other organisms.
This matters enormously when you are trying to commercialize a product.

Yeast grows a little slower than E. coli — doubling time around 90 minutes — and is a little more demanding nutritionally, but it scales up cleanly to enormous fermenters, and the world's brewing industry has built out a hundred years of know-how on how to handle yeast at scale.
When you want to engineer a complex eukaryotic biosynthetic pathway — particularly anything involving plant or fungal natural products — yeast is, more often than not, the platform of choice.
This will matter when we get to artemisinin.

The third workhorse is **Bacillus subtilis** — a gram-positive soil bacterium, also harmless, well-characterized, and remarkably good at one thing in particular: secreting proteins into the growth medium.

Where E. coli tends to keep its proteins inside the cell, and you have to lyse the cells to get the product out, Bacillus will happily push proteins out through its envelope into the surrounding liquid, where you can simply spin down the cells and harvest the supernatant.
This is a huge advantage for industrial enzyme production.
A vast amount of the world's industrial enzymes — proteases for laundry detergent, amylases for high-fructose corn syrup, lipases for cheese — are made by engineered Bacillus strains.
The industrial fermenters that crank out these enzymes are some of the largest biotechnology operations in the world, often tens or hundreds of thousands of liters, and they all run on Bacillus.
The fourth, and the one you may not have heard of, is **Yarrowia lipolytica**.
Yarrowia is what is called an oleaginous yeast — a yeast that naturally accumulates large amounts of lipid, sometimes up to 40 percent of its dry mass as oil.
That makes it the natural platform for engineering anything in the lipid family — biofuels, fatty acids, lipid-soluble vitamins, omega-3s, specialty oils.
Yarrowia has been used commercially for things like microbial omega-3 oil for infant formula.
As biofuels and oil-based chemicals become a bigger fraction of the metabolic engineering portfolio, Yarrowia has become more and more important.
It is the lipid specialist of the workhorse cast.
There are others, with narrower roles.
Pichia pastoris**, another yeast, now formally renamed Komagataella phaffii, is a methanol-fed yeast that has become a favorite for secreted recombinant proteins.
Corynebacterium glutamicum** is the workhorse of the amino acid industry — most of the world's industrial glutamate and lysine are made by engineered Corynebacterium.
Streptomyces species, the filamentous bacteria that naturally make a vast number of antibiotics, are engineered to produce novel antibiotic variants and other complex natural products.
Aspergillus niger**, a filamentous fungus, makes citric acid at industrial scale — the citric acid in your soda comes from engineered Aspergillus.
Chinese hamster ovary cells**, the mammalian cell line known as CHO, is the workhorse for therapeutic antibodies, although that is more cell-culture biology than classical metabolic engineering.
The list goes on, but the four I listed first — E. coli, S. cerevisiae, Bacillus, Yarrowia — cover most of what we will talk about today.
The analogy I want you to carry: **each workhorse is a different shop floor, with a different layout, different unionized labor, different ventilation.
You match the workhorse to the product.
Simple bacterial proteins and small molecules go to E. coli.
Complex eukaryotic natural products and secreted glycoproteins go to yeast.
Massive secreted industrial enzymes go to Bacillus.

Lipids and oils go to Yarrowia.** Picking the workhorse is the first major engineering decision, and it constrains everything downstream — the toolkit you can use, the media you can grow on, the regulatory pathway, the scale-up economics.
Get it wrong, and no amount of clever genetic engineering will save you.
Now let us look at the first generation of products this discipline made, because the history actually frames a lot of what came later.
